summary: I was born in Long Beach, California in 1960 at Saint Mary's hospital. Although I was born there, my family moved to Wilmington in 1942, so I spent most of my life around the harbor area. Growing up in Long Beach during the sixties shaped a lot of my early experiences.

My parents moved to Wilmington in 1942, and I've lived there my entire life. Wilmington is the heart of the harbor and the port town, and it's where my family and I have deep roots. Even today, I still live in Wilmington, surrounded by the community that shaped my upbringing.

My father and brothers took part in building the Vincent Thomas Bridge, which was one of the many projects my family worked on as ironworkers. It’s a significant part of our legacy in the Wilmington and harbor area. Their work on that bridge is something I’m proud to be connected to through my family.

Along with the Vincent Thomas Bridge, my father and brothers also helped build the Gerald Desmond Bridge. These projects were major undertakings in the harbor area, and my family’s involvement showed their dedication to the ironworking trade. It’s part of the history I grew up with in Wilmington.

entity: Mary Star of the Sea High School
summary: I attended Mary Star of the Sea, a Catholic high school located in San Pedro, California. This school was where I completed my secondary education before moving on to college and eventually joining the workforce. It was an important step in my early life in the Los Angeles harbor area.

San Pedro is where I went to high school at Mary Star of the Sea. It’s part of the greater Los Angeles harbor area, close to where I grew up in Wilmington. The community there was part of my formative years before I entered college and the workforce.

At 18, I joined my first union, the Retail Clerks Union, Union 770, in Harbor City. It was a big deal to become a journeyman in that union while working part-time at a grocery store. I stayed with them for a couple of years while attending college, appreciating the benefits and organized labor structure.

I attended Harbor College in Wilmington after high school. While there, I balanced college classes with working part-time jobs and union membership. It was during this time that my brothers encouraged me to consider joining the ironworkers, which changed my career path.

I drove my sister to California State University, Dominguez Hills, where she earned her master’s degree despite her severe disabilities. This was a significant part of my life as I helped care for her and supported her educational goals. It showed me the importance of responsibility and perseverance.

My father owned A-1 Erection, a structural steel company based in Wilmington, California. My brothers and father worked there and were involved in major projects like the Vincent Thomas and Gerald Desmond Bridges. The company was a big part of our family’s livelihood and connection to the ironworking trade.

I became the first female ironworker in the Southern California-Nevada area in 1981, breaking into a male-dominated field. My father and brothers were ironworkers, so it was a family tradition, though my father was initially upset about me joining. I went through a rigorous apprenticeship and eventually became top of my class, proud to carry on the ironworker legacy.

My father came to California from Nebraska in 1938. He initially worked in the oil fields before briefly working on the docks. His move from Nebraska was the start of our family’s long history in the California labor and union community.

My first ironworking job placement was at Champlin Oil Refinery in Wilmington, which is now located in Vallejo, California. I spent about a year there, starting in the boneyard making shims before moving into the raising gang. It was a tough environment with thousands of men, but it was where I proved myself.

Champlin Refining and Chemicals, where I worked as an ironworker, is now located in Vallejo, California. Although I worked at the Wilmington site, the company’s relocation to Vallejo is part of its history. Vallejo represents the broader geographic context of the refinery’s operations.

When I started in the ironworkers raising gang, my foreman asked if I had ever been on the E ride at Disneyland as a way to introduce me to working at heights. They used a basket made by ironworkers to lift me up in a crane, which was both thrilling and intimidating. It was a memorable first experience on the job.

My father had a big yard down by Local 13 in Wilmington where we would weld and make things together. Local 13 was a hub for ironworkers in the area and a place where I connected with my family and the trade. It was a special part of my ironworking life.

I am affiliated with Local 63, which is part of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union. My involvement with Local 63 connects me to the broader labor movement in the harbor area. It’s one of the unions I have been part of during my career.

I am a member of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union, specifically Local 13 and Local 63. This union represents workers in the harbor and waterfront areas where I have lived and worked. Being part of the ILWU connects me to a strong labor tradition in the Pacific Coast ports.

While I didn’t directly work with the Teamsters, they are part of the labor landscape in the harbor area alongside unions like the ILWU and ironworkers. My family and I were more connected to the ironworkers and ILWU, but the Teamsters are another important union in this region.

The Golden v. Pacific Maritime Association case was mentioned in the context of union activities and labor rights, which are part of the environment I have been involved in through the ILWU and other unions. While I did not discuss it in detail, it represents important legal history affecting maritime workers like myself.

I participated in the Pacific Coast Pensioners Association convention in Vancouver in 2014, which was part of preserving and sharing the history of labor on the coast. Being involved with the PCPA connects me to retired workers and the legacy of unions like the ILWU. It’s a way to honor the struggles and achievements of workers over the years.
